title: Patterns, Sequences & Series
blurb: Arithmetic and geometric sequences and series, quadratic number patterns, sigma notation and sum to infinity.

parts.q: Determine a formula for $T_n$, the $n$-th term of the sequence.
Which term of the sequence is equal to $85$?
Calculate the sum of the first $20$ terms of the sequence.

questions.context: The quadratic number pattern $6;\ 17;\ 34;\ 57;\ \ldots$ has a constant second difference.

parts.q: Write down the next term of the pattern.
solution: First differences: $11;\ 17;\ 23$, second difference $6$, so the next first difference is $29$

parts.q: Determine a formula for the $n$-th term of the pattern in the form $T_n=an^2+bn+c$.
Determine which term of the pattern is the FIRST term greater than $500$.
solution: So $n=13$ is the first natural number that works: $T_{13}=3(169)+26+1=534>500$ (and $T_{12}=457<500$)

questions.context: In a geometric sequence, the second term is $6$ and the fifth term is $48$.

parts.q: Determine the first term $a$ and the common ratio $r$ of the sequence.
Calculate the sum of the first $10$ terms of the sequence.
Calculate: $\sum_{k=1}^{15}(3k-5)$.
solution: The terms form an arithmetic series: first term $3(1)-5=-2$, last term $3(15)-5=40$, with $15$ terms

parts.q: Write the series $3+6+12+\ldots+384$ in sigma notation.
Determine the values of $x$ for which the series converges.
solution: $1<x<3$, with $x\neq 2$ (at $x=2$ every term is $0$, so the series is not geometric)

parts.q: Calculate the sum to infinity of the series if $x=2{,}5$.

questions.context: The numbers $x;\ x+4;\ 2x+2$ are the first three terms of a geometric sequence.

parts.q: Determine the possible value(s) of $x$.
If $x=8$, determine the $10$th term of the sequence.
If $x=-2$, explain why the sum to infinity of the sequence does NOT exist.
solution: $S_\infty$ exists only when $-1<r<1$; since $r=-1$ the series does not converge
parts.answer: $r=-1$, which does not satisfy $-1<r<1$, so the series does not converge

questions.context: In an arithmetic sequence, the third term is $11$ and the eighth term is $31$.

parts.q: Determine the first term and the common difference of the sequence.
Which term of the sequence is equal to $99$?
Calculate the sum of the first $25$ terms of the sequence.
Calculate: $\sum_{k=3}^{20}(5k+2)$.
solution: Arithmetic series: first term $5(3)+2=17$, last term $5(20)+2=102$

parts.q: Write the series $2+9+16+\ldots+142$ in sigma notation.

questions.context: A quadratic number pattern has first term $T_1=2$. The FIRST differences of the pattern form the arithmetic sequence $5;\ 9;\ 13;\ \ldots$

parts.q: Write down the values of $T_2$, $T_3$ and $T_4$.
Determine a formula for the $n$-th term of the pattern in the form $T_n=an^2+bn+c$.
Calculate $T_{40}-T_{39}$ WITHOUT calculating $T_{40}$ or $T_{39}$ themselves.
solution: $T_{40}-T_{39}$ is the $39$th first difference of the pattern
The first differences are arithmetic with first term $5$ and common difference $4$: the $k$th first difference is $5+4(k-1)=4k+1$

questions.context: The numbers $4;\ x;\ y$ are the first three terms of an arithmetic sequence, and $x;\ y;\ 18$ are the first three terms of a geometric sequence.

parts.q: Determine the value(s) of $x$ and $y$.
solution: If $x=8$ then $y=12$ (geometric: $8;\ 12;\ 18$ with $r=\dfrac{3}{2}$); if $x=\dfrac{1}{2}$ then $y=-3$ (geometric: $\dfrac{1}{2};\ -3;\ 18$ with $r=-6$) — both are valid

parts.q: If $x=8$, calculate the sum of the first $12$ terms of the ARITHMETIC sequence.
solution: The arithmetic sequence is $4;\ 8;\ 12;\ \ldots$ with $a=4$ and $d=4$

questions.context: A pendulum at a science centre in Johannesburg is released. Its first swing has an arc length of $60$ cm, and each swing after that has an arc length that is $85\%$ of the previous swing.

parts.q: Calculate the arc length of the fifth swing.
Determine which swing is the FIRST one with an arc length of less than $10$ cm.
Calculate the TOTAL distance the pendulum's tip travels if it is left to swing indefinitely.
solution: $r=0{,}85$ satisfies $-1<r<1$, so the sum to infinity exists
